facts.label: SANON SILENCES DINO ZOFF
facts.story: Twenty-two-year-old Emmanuel Sanon played for amateur club Don Bosco, but on 15 June 1974 in Munich he ran onto a Philippe Vorbe through ball, rounded Italy's legendary Dino Zoff, and slotted into an empty net in the 46th minute. It ended Zoff's streak of 1,142 minutes without conceding — a World Cup and European record. Haiti led group favorites Italy 1–0 before their inexperience showed; Italy scored twice in six minutes and won 3–1, but Sanon's strike remains Haitian football's most cherished moment.

facts.label: A DOPING SCANDAL IN MUNICH
facts.story: Haiti's World Cup debut was shadowed by controversy. After the Italy match, defender Ernst Jean-Joseph failed a drug test — he said he'd taken asthma pills — and was expelled from the tournament by FIFA. Reports from the era claimed Haiti's ruler Jean-Claude 'Baby Doc' Duvalier had Jean-Joseph forced to call his teammates and prove he was still alive before their final match against Argentina, a chilling footnote to a tournament otherwise remembered for Sanon's heroics.

1974.headline: Sanon Stuns Dino Zoff

annotations.label: BREAKING THE UNBEATEN STREAK
annotations.story: In just their second-ever World Cup match, Haiti stunned Italy when Emmanuel Sanon rounded goalkeeper Dino Zoff in the 46th minute — ending Zoff's 1,142-minute shutout streak, a World Cup record. Haiti led 1–0 before Italy responded with two quick goals and won 3–1, but the moment made Sanon a national hero.

annotations.label: OVERWHELMED BY POLAND
annotations.story: Three days after their heroics against Italy, Haiti was routed 7–0 by an unstoppable Poland side that would go on to finish third in the tournament. It remains the heaviest defeat in Haitian World Cup history and a stark reminder of the gap between debutants and the era's best.

annotations.label: SANON'S SECOND MASTERPIECE
annotations.story: Already eliminated, Haiti gave Argentina a scare when Emmanuel Sanon struck again in the 63rd minute, becoming the only Haitian to ever score twice at a World Cup. Argentina answered with four goals of their own, winning 4–1, but Sanon closed his country's debut exactly how he'd opened it — as its brightest star.

1974.homeColors: red shirt with black trim, echoing the red-and-black national flag adopted under the Duvalier government
